questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between a project and operations?
options: A project is temporary with a unique deliverable; operations are ongoing and repetitive
A project has no budget; operations always have a budget
A project involves only one person; operations involve teams
A project never has a deadline; operations always have deadlines
questions.explanation: A project is a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result, while operations are ongoing, repetitive activities that sustain an organization. The other options misrepresent both concepts.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Project Management Office (PMO)?
options: To standardize project management practices and provide governance across the organization
To replace the project manager on all projects
To handle only the financial aspects of projects
To serve as the sole communication channel between stakeholders
questions.explanation: A PMO standardizes project management practices, provides governance, offers templates and methodologies, and supports project managers across the organization. It does not replace project managers or handle only finances.

questions.question: In a matrix organizational structure, a project team member reports to both a functional manager and a project manager. Who typically controls the team member's performance review?
options: The functional manager, though the project manager may provide input
questions.explanation: In a matrix organization, the functional manager retains authority over performance reviews and administrative matters, while the project manager directs project work. The project manager may provide input on performance, but the functional manager typically owns the review process.

questions.question: Which organizational structure gives the project manager the MOST authority over project resources?
questions.explanation: In a projectized organization, the project manager has full authority over resources, budget, and team assignments. Functional structures give the least authority to PMs, and matrix structures fall in between depending on whether they are weak, balanced, or strong.

questions.question: The triple constraint in project management consists of which three elements?
questions.explanation: The triple constraint (also called the iron triangle) consists of scope, time, and cost. Changes to any one element will impact the others. Quality is often shown at the center of the triangle as it is affected by all three constraints.

questions.question: A project sponsor demands that the project scope be increased without extending the deadline or increasing the budget. What is the MOST likely impact?
options: The project team will become more productive
questions.explanation: According to the triple constraint, if scope increases while time and cost remain fixed, quality is the most likely area to suffer. Adding more work without additional time or money forces trade-offs that typically degrade deliverable quality.

questions.question: Who is ultimately responsible for the success or failure of a project?
questions.explanation: The project manager is ultimately responsible for the success or failure of a project. While the project sponsor provides funding and strategic direction, and the team executes the work, the PM is accountable for planning, executing, and delivering the project outcomes.

questions.question: A project sponsor has been disengaged throughout the project. During the final review, the sponsor rejects the deliverables. What should the project manager have done differently?
options: Escalated the sponsor's disengagement early and ensured regular stakeholder engagement
Completed the project without the sponsor's input to save time
Replaced the sponsor with a team lead
Delayed the project until the sponsor became available
questions.explanation: The PM should have escalated the lack of sponsor engagement early and implemented a stakeholder engagement strategy to get regular sign-offs. Waiting for the final review to get sponsor feedback creates significant risk of rework or rejection.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes a stakeholder in a project?
options: Only the individuals who fund the project
Anyone who may be positively or negatively affected by the project or its outcome
Only the project team members who do the work
The customer who receives the final deliverable
questions.explanation: A stakeholder is any individual, group, or organization that may be positively or negatively affected by the project or its outcome. This includes sponsors, team members, customers, end users, and even competitors or regulatory bodies.

questions.question: A project manager discovers that a key stakeholder who was not identified during planning is now opposing the project. What is the BEST course of action?
options: Ignore the stakeholder since they were not in the original stakeholder register
Update the stakeholder register, assess their influence and interest, and develop an engagement strategy
Immediately change the project scope to accommodate their concerns
Escalate to the PMO and halt all project work
questions.explanation: Stakeholder identification is an ongoing process. When a new stakeholder is discovered, the PM should update the stakeholder register, assess their power/interest/influence, and develop an appropriate engagement strategy to address their concerns without derailing the project.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a project?
options: It has a definite beginning and end
It produces the same output repeatedly
questions.explanation: A project is temporary in nature, meaning it has a definite start and end date. Ongoing, repetitive work describes operations, not projects. All projects require resources to execute.

questions.question: In a strong matrix organization, a conflict arises between a functional manager and a project manager over resource allocation. Who typically has the greater authority to resolve this?
options: The functional manager, since they own the resource
The project manager, since the strong matrix favors project authority
The PMO, since they arbitrate all resource conflicts
The team member, since they decide where to work
questions.explanation: In a strong matrix organization, the project manager has greater authority than the functional manager regarding project resource allocation. This is the key distinction between weak, balanced, and strong matrix structures: the balance of power shifts toward the PM in strong matrix.

questions.question: A project is running over budget. The project manager decides to reduce the scope to bring costs back in line. This is an example of managing which concept?
questions.explanation: Adjusting scope to manage cost is a direct example of the triple constraint in action. When one constraint (cost) is under pressure, the PM may adjust another constraint (scope) to maintain balance. While change control may be involved in the process, the underlying concept is the triple constraint.

questions.question: What is project governance?
options: The framework of authority, accountability, and decision-making for a project
The daily task assignments given by the project manager
The process of writing project documentation
The method of calculating project costs
questions.explanation: Project governance is the framework that defines authority, accountability, and decision-making structures for a project. It establishes who can make which decisions, escalation paths, and oversight mechanisms. It is not about daily task management or documentation.

questions.question: A steering committee reviews project progress quarterly and makes go/no-go decisions. This is an example of which concept?
questions.explanation: A steering committee providing oversight and making go/no-go decisions is a classic example of project governance. Governance ensures projects align with organizational strategy and have proper oversight through defined decision-making processes.

questions.question: What is the primary role of the project sponsor?
options: To provide funding, support, and strategic direction for the project
To perform quality testing on deliverables
questions.explanation: The project sponsor provides funding, organizational support, and strategic direction. They champion the project at the executive level, remove organizational barriers, and approve major deliverables. Day-to-day management is the project manager's responsibility.

questions.question: Which project selection method compares the present value of cash inflows to the initial investment?
questions.explanation: Net present value (NPV) calculates the present value of all future cash inflows minus the initial investment. A positive NPV indicates the project is financially viable. Payback period measures time to recoup investment, IRR calculates the discount rate at which NPV equals zero, and cost-benefit analysis is a broader comparison.

questions.question: A project has a fixed deadline that cannot change. The customer requests additional features. What should the project manager do FIRST?
options: Add the features and accept the overtime cost
Assess the impact on scope, cost, and quality, then present options to the sponsor
Extend the deadline without informing the customer
questions.explanation: The PM should first assess the impact of the requested changes on all constraints (scope, cost, quality) since time is fixed. Then present options with trade-offs to the sponsor for a decision. Rejecting outright or accepting without analysis are both inappropriate responses.

questions.question: A power/interest grid is used to classify stakeholders. A stakeholder with high power and low interest should be managed how?
questions.explanation: On a power/interest grid, stakeholders with high power but low interest should be kept satisfied. They have the ability to impact the project but are not actively engaged. Over-communicating may annoy them; under-communicating risks them becoming a blocker when they do engage.

questions.question: In a functional organizational structure, who has the MOST authority over project resources?
questions.explanation: In a functional organizational structure, the functional manager has the most authority over resources. Project managers in functional organizations have very limited authority and often serve more as coordinators than managers.

questions.question: An organization must choose between two projects. Project A has an NPV of $50,000 and a payback period of 3 years. Project B has an NPV of $35,000 and a payback period of 1 year. If the organization prioritizes long-term value, which project should be selected?
options: Project A, because it has a higher NPV
Project B, because it has a shorter payback period
Both projects, because they are both profitable
Neither project, because more information is needed
questions.explanation: If long-term value is the priority, Project A should be selected because it has a higher NPV ($50,000 vs $35,000). NPV measures total value over the project's life. Payback period only measures how quickly the initial investment is recovered, which favors Project B but does not reflect total value.

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST example of a gate review in project governance?
options: A daily standup meeting with the development team
A formal decision point where stakeholders decide whether the project should proceed to the next phase
A weekly status email sent to the project team
A retrospective meeting at the end of a sprint
questions.explanation: A gate review (also called a phase gate or stage gate) is a formal governance checkpoint where key stakeholders evaluate project progress and decide whether to continue, modify, or terminate the project before it moves to the next phase. Daily standups and status emails are communication mechanisms, not governance decision points.

questions.question: Which project role is responsible for providing subject matter expertise to the project team?
questions.explanation: A subject matter expert (SME) provides specialized knowledge and expertise in a specific area relevant to the project. The project sponsor provides funding and direction, the coordinator handles administrative tasks, and the Scrum master facilitates Agile processes.

questions.question: If a project's scope remains constant but the timeline is compressed, which constraint is MOST likely to be affected?
options: Cost will increase due to the need for additional resources
Quality will improve because of faster delivery
questions.explanation: When the timeline is compressed while scope remains fixed, cost typically increases because additional resources (overtime, more staff, expedited materials) are needed to complete the same work in less time. This is a direct application of the triple constraint.

questions.question: A stakeholder has been providing informal requirements directly to developers, bypassing the project manager. What is the MOST appropriate action?
options: Allow it to continue since the stakeholder is important
Remove the stakeholder from the project
Reinforce the change control process and redirect all requirements through proper channels
Add all informal requirements to the project scope immediately
questions.explanation: The PM should reinforce the formal change control process and ensure all requirements flow through proper channels. Allowing informal requirements leads to scope creep and uncontrolled changes. The stakeholder should not be removed but rather educated on the correct process.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a program rather than a project?
options: A collection of related projects managed together to achieve strategic benefits
questions.explanation: A program is a group of related projects managed in a coordinated manner to achieve benefits and control not available from managing them individually. The other options describe individual projects. Programs provide strategic alignment and resource optimization across multiple related projects.

questions.question: In which organizational structure do team members work in departments organized by specialty, such as engineering, marketing, and finance?
questions.explanation: In a functional organizational structure, team members are grouped by specialty or department (engineering, marketing, finance, etc.). The functional manager has primary authority over resources and the project manager has limited influence.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a project governance board?
options: To write code for the project deliverables
To provide oversight, strategic alignment, and decision-making authority for the project
To replace the project manager during vacations
questions.explanation: A project governance board provides strategic oversight, ensures projects align with organizational objectives, makes key decisions (such as go/no-go at phase gates), and resolves escalated issues. They are not involved in daily execution or technical work.

questions.question: A project manager realizes that the project team lacks a critical skill needed to complete a deliverable. Which is the BEST approach?
options: Acquire the resource externally or train an existing team member, and update the resource management plan
Skip the deliverable and hope no one notices
Reassign the work to the project sponsor
questions.explanation: When a skill gap is identified, the PM should evaluate options such as hiring, contracting, or training and then update the resource management plan accordingly. Canceling the project or skipping deliverables are disproportionate responses, and sponsors do not perform project work.

questions.question: What document identifies all project stakeholders, their interests, and their level of influence?
questions.explanation: The stakeholder register documents all identified stakeholders along with their interests, influence, engagement levels, and communication needs. The project charter authorizes the project, the WBS breaks down scope, and the risk register tracks project risks.

questions.question: What is the difference between a portfolio and a program?
options: A portfolio is a collection of projects and programs managed together to achieve strategic objectives; a program is a group of related projects
A portfolio and a program are the same concept
A portfolio is smaller in scope than a single project
questions.explanation: A portfolio is the highest-level grouping, containing projects, programs, and other work managed together to achieve strategic business objectives. A program is a group of related projects managed in a coordinated way. Portfolios focus on strategic alignment; programs focus on coordinated benefits.

questions.question: A project has experienced scope creep that has increased costs by 20%. The sponsor refuses to provide additional funding. What is the project manager's BEST option?
options: Continue spending and hope the sponsor changes their mind
Negotiate a scope reduction with stakeholders to bring the project back within budget
Reduce team size to cut costs, regardless of impact on deliverables
questions.explanation: When budget is fixed and scope has expanded, the PM should negotiate with stakeholders to reduce scope back to a level that fits within the approved budget. This is a practical application of the triple constraint, trading scope to maintain cost. Continuing to overspend or abruptly ending the project are not appropriate responses.

questions.question: What does an escalation path define in project governance?
options: The order in which tasks should be completed
The chain of authority for resolving issues that cannot be resolved at the project level
The physical layout of the project office
The technical architecture of the project deliverable
questions.explanation: An escalation path defines the chain of authority and the process for elevating issues that cannot be resolved at lower levels. It ensures that blockers and critical decisions reach the appropriate decision-makers in a timely manner.

questions.question: What is a key disadvantage of a projectized organizational structure?
options: The project manager has no authority
Team members may lack job security since they are reassigned after projects end
Communication between projects is always excellent
questions.explanation: In projectized organizations, team members are dedicated to projects and may face uncertainty about their next assignment when a project ends. This can affect morale and retention. The project manager actually has maximum authority in this structure, which is an advantage, not a disadvantage.

questions.question: Which role is responsible for removing impediments for the team in a Scrum framework?
questions.explanation: The Scrum master is responsible for removing impediments (blockers) that prevent the development team from making progress. The product owner manages the backlog, the sponsor provides funding, and the business analyst gathers requirements.

questions.question: On a power/interest grid, a stakeholder with low power and high interest should be managed by which strategy?
questions.explanation: Stakeholders with low power but high interest should be kept informed. They are engaged and care about the project but lack the authority to significantly impact it. Regular updates and transparent communication keep them supportive without requiring intensive management.

questions.question: An organization is evaluating three projects. Project X has a benefit-cost ratio (BCR) of 2.5, Project Y has a BCR of 1.8, and Project Z has a BCR of 0.9. Which project(s) should be rejected?
questions.explanation: A benefit-cost ratio (BCR) below 1.0 means the costs exceed the benefits, making the project financially unviable. Project Z (BCR 0.9) should be rejected. Projects X and Y both have BCRs above 1.0, indicating positive returns, with Project X being the most favorable.

questions.question: What is the term for uncontrolled changes or continuous growth in a project's scope without corresponding adjustments to time, cost, and resources?
questions.explanation: Scope creep refers to uncontrolled expansion of project scope without adjustments to time, cost, or resources. Gold plating is adding extra features not requested by the customer. Scope validation is the formal acceptance of deliverables. Progressive elaboration is the iterative refinement of project details.

questions.question: A project governance framework requires that all projects exceeding $500,000 receive quarterly executive reviews. This is an example of which governance element?
questions.explanation: Setting financial thresholds that trigger specific oversight activities (such as executive reviews) is a governance mechanism that ensures appropriate oversight based on project size and risk. This establishes clear criteria for when enhanced governance controls apply.

questions.question: Which role is responsible for defining the business need and ensuring the project delivers value to the organization?
questions.explanation: The project sponsor defines the business need, provides funding, and ensures the project delivers organizational value. The business analyst may help articulate requirements, but the sponsor owns the business case and is accountable for the value delivered.

questions.question: What is stakeholder analysis?
options: A financial audit of stakeholder investments
The process of identifying stakeholders and assessing their interest, influence, and potential impact on the project
A legal review of stakeholder contracts
A technical assessment of stakeholder systems
questions.explanation: Stakeholder analysis is the process of identifying all project stakeholders and systematically assessing their interests, influence, expectations, and potential impact on the project. This analysis informs the stakeholder engagement and communication strategies.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes a business case?
options: A document justifying the investment in a project by outlining expected benefits, costs, and risks
A list of project team members and their roles
A technical specification for the project deliverable
questions.explanation: A business case is a documented justification for undertaking a project. It outlines the expected benefits, costs, risks, and alternatives, providing decision-makers with the information needed to approve or reject the project investment.

questions.question: A company uses a weak matrix structure. Project managers are finding it difficult to get resources committed to their projects. What would be the MOST effective organizational change?
options: Move to a purely functional structure
Move to a balanced or strong matrix structure to give project managers more authority
questions.explanation: In a weak matrix, PMs have little authority over resources. Moving to a balanced or strong matrix gives PMs more authority to negotiate for and commit resources, addressing the core issue. Moving to a functional structure would make the problem worse.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the triple constraint elements?
questions.explanation: The triple constraint consists of scope, time, and cost. While quality is closely related and often shown at the center of the triangle (affected by changes to any of the three), it is not one of the three primary constraints itself.

questions.question: A project manager needs approval from the governance board before proceeding past the design phase. This approval point is known as a:
questions.explanation: A phase gate (also called a stage gate or kill point) is a formal governance decision point where the project is reviewed and a go/no-go decision is made before proceeding to the next phase. Milestones mark progress but do not necessarily require formal approval.

questions.question: In a RACI matrix, only one person can be designated as which role for each task?
questions.explanation: In a RACI matrix, only one person should be designated as Accountable for each task. This ensures clear ownership and avoids confusion about who has final decision-making authority. Multiple people can be Responsible, Consulted, or Informed, but accountability must be singular.

questions.question: Which stakeholder engagement level describes a stakeholder who is aware of the project but neither supportive nor resistant?
questions.explanation: A neutral stakeholder is aware of the project but has not taken a position for or against it. The engagement levels typically range from unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, to leading. The PM's goal is usually to move stakeholders toward supportive engagement.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the concept of opportunity cost in project selection?
options: The cost of the selected project
The value of the next best alternative that is foregone when a project is selected
The total budget allocated to all projects
The overhead cost of the PMO
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative that must be given up when choosing one project over another. For example, if selecting Project A means Project B cannot be done, the opportunity cost of selecting Project A is the value that Project B would have provided.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of a matrix organizational structure?
options: Efficient sharing of resources across multiple projects
questions.explanation: A key advantage of matrix structures is that resources can be shared efficiently across multiple projects while still maintaining their functional expertise. The trade-off is potential role conflict from dual reporting. It does not eliminate conflict or remove the need for functional managers.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a tollgate review?
options: To calculate project costs at each milestone
To formally evaluate whether a project meets predefined criteria before advancing to the next phase
To assign new team members mid-project
questions.explanation: A tollgate review (synonymous with phase gate or stage gate) is a formal evaluation point where the project must demonstrate it meets predefined criteria before being allowed to proceed. This is a governance mechanism that prevents projects from advancing prematurely.

questions.question: Which document formally authorizes the existence of a project and provides the project manager with authority to apply organizational resources?
questions.explanation: The project charter formally authorizes the project and gives the project manager authority to use organizational resources. It is created during the initiation phase. The project management plan, WBS, and SOW are created during planning or pre-project phases.

questions.question: During project initiation, which of the following activities should be performed?
options: Identifying the project sponsor, defining high-level scope, and developing the business case
questions.explanation: During initiation, the focus is on defining the project at a high level: identifying the sponsor, establishing the business case, defining high-level scope, identifying stakeholders, and creating the project charter. Detailed scheduling occurs in planning; quality audits in execution; and resource release in closing.

questions.question: What is a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)?
options: A hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team
A list of risks sorted by probability
A financial report showing project expenses
questions.explanation: A WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of the total project scope into manageable work packages. It organizes and defines the total scope of the project and provides the basis for planning, estimating, scheduling, and controlling project work.

questions.question: Which planning activity involves identifying the sequence of activities and determining the longest path through the project network?
questions.explanation: The critical path method (CPM) involves sequencing project activities and calculating the longest path through the network diagram. The critical path determines the shortest possible project duration. Any delay on a critical path activity directly delays the project end date.

questions.question: A project manager is developing the project budget and wants to include a reserve for unknown risks. What type of reserve should be used?
questions.explanation: Management reserve is set aside for unknown risks (unknown unknowns) and is typically controlled by senior management. Contingency reserve is for known risks (known unknowns) that have been identified and analyzed. Management reserve is not part of the project baseline but is part of the total project budget.

questions.question: During which project phase is the actual work performed to create the project deliverables?
questions.explanation: The execution phase is when the project team performs the work defined in the project management plan to produce the project deliverables. Initiation authorizes the project, planning defines how it will be done, and closing formally completes it.

questions.question: A project manager notices that team productivity is declining during execution. Which is the BEST first action?
options: Investigate the root cause by speaking with team members and reviewing workload
Report the team to senior management
questions.explanation: The PM should first investigate the root cause of declining productivity. This could be due to unclear requirements, resource overload, team conflict, or other factors. Replacing the entire team is drastic and counterproductive; extending deadlines or escalating without understanding the cause are premature responses.

questions.question: Earned Value Management (EVM) uses three key metrics. Which metric represents the budgeted cost of work actually performed?
questions.explanation: Earned Value (EV) represents the budgeted cost of work actually performed. Planned Value (PV) is the budgeted cost of work scheduled. Actual Cost (AC) is the actual money spent. Cost Variance is a calculated metric (EV - AC), not a base measurement.

questions.question: A project has an EV of $100,000, a PV of $120,000, and an AC of $110,000. What can be determined about the project?
options: The project is ahead of schedule and under budget
The project is behind schedule and over budget
The project is ahead of schedule and over budget
The project is behind schedule and under budget
questions.explanation: Schedule Variance (SV) = EV - PV = $100,000 - $120,000 = -$20,000 (behind schedule). Cost Variance (CV) = EV - AC = $100,000 - $110,000 = -$10,000 (over budget). Both variances are negative, meaning the project is both behind schedule and over budget.

questions.question: During monitoring and controlling, the project manager compares actual performance against the project baseline. This is known as:
questions.explanation: Variance analysis compares actual project performance against the project baseline (scope, schedule, cost) to identify deviations. This is a fundamental monitoring and controlling activity that helps the PM determine if corrective actions are needed.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key activity during the closing phase of a project?
options: Conducting lessons learned and archiving project documents
questions.explanation: During the closing phase, key activities include conducting lessons learned sessions, archiving project documents, obtaining formal acceptance of deliverables, releasing resources, and closing out contracts. Creating the WBS and charter occur during earlier phases.

questions.question: Why is it important to conduct a lessons learned session at the end of a project?
options: To assign blame for problems that occurred
To capture what went well and what could be improved for future projects
To calculate the final project cost
questions.explanation: Lessons learned sessions capture both successes and areas for improvement so that future projects can benefit from the experience. They are not about assigning blame but about organizational learning and continuous improvement.

questions.question: A project has four activities: A (5 days), B (3 days), C (7 days), and D (2 days). Activity A and B can start simultaneously. Activity C depends on A. Activity D depends on B. What is the critical path duration?
questions.explanation: Path 1: A (5) + C (7) = 12 days. Path 2: B (3) + D (2) = 5 days. The critical path is the longest path: A-C at 12 days. This is the minimum project duration, and any delay on A or C will delay the project.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a project baseline?
options: To provide a fixed reference point against which project performance is measured
To define the project's organizational structure
questions.explanation: A project baseline (scope, schedule, and cost baselines) serves as a fixed reference point against which actual project performance is compared. Changes to baselines must go through formal change control. Baselines are essential for variance analysis during monitoring and controlling.

questions.question: Which estimating technique uses historical data from similar projects to estimate the current project's duration or cost?
questions.explanation: Analogous estimating uses historical data from similar past projects to estimate the current project. It is less accurate than bottom-up or parametric methods but is faster and useful when limited project information is available, such as during initiation.

questions.question: A project manager uses three-point estimating with the following values for an activity: Optimistic = 4 days, Most Likely = 6 days, Pessimistic = 14 days. Using the PERT formula, what is the expected duration?
questions.explanation: The PERT formula is (Optimistic + 4 x Most Likely + Pessimistic) / 6 = (4 + 24 + 14) / 6 = 42 / 6 = 7 days. The PERT formula weights the most likely estimate four times more heavily than the optimistic and pessimistic values.

questions.question: During execution, the project manager discovers that a vendor's deliverable does not meet the agreed-upon quality standards. What should the PM do FIRST?
options: Accept the deliverable to avoid schedule delays
Document the deficiency and work with the vendor to implement corrective actions per the contract
Ignore the issue and test in the next phase
questions.explanation: The PM should document the quality deficiency and work with the vendor to resolve it according to the terms of the contract. Accepting substandard work, immediately canceling the contract, or ignoring the issue are all inappropriate responses that could create larger problems.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a status report during monitoring and controlling?
options: To communicate project progress, issues, and risks to stakeholders
questions.explanation: Status reports communicate current project progress, upcoming milestones, issues, risks, and any deviations from the plan to stakeholders. They are a key tool during the monitoring and controlling phase for keeping stakeholders informed and enabling timely decisions.

questions.question: What is resource leveling?
options: Adding more resources to a project to speed it up
Adjusting the project schedule to resolve resource over-allocation without changing the project scope
Removing resources from a project to cut costs
Assigning the same resource to all tasks
questions.explanation: Resource leveling adjusts the project schedule to resolve resource conflicts and over-allocations. It may extend the project timeline to ensure no resource is assigned more work than they can handle. This differs from resource smoothing, which adjusts within float without extending the timeline.

questions.question: A project manager needs to compress the schedule without changing the project scope. Which technique adds resources to critical path activities?
questions.explanation: Crashing adds resources to critical path activities to shorten the schedule, typically at increased cost. Fast tracking performs activities in parallel that were originally planned sequentially, which increases risk. Resource leveling may actually extend the schedule, and lead/lag adjusts activity relationships.

questions.question: What is the difference between fast tracking and crashing?
options: Fast tracking adds resources; crashing performs activities in parallel
Fast tracking performs sequential activities in parallel; crashing adds resources to shorten duration
They are the same technique with different names
Fast tracking extends the schedule; crashing reduces scope
questions.explanation: Fast tracking performs activities in parallel that were originally planned sequentially, which increases risk but does not necessarily increase cost. Crashing adds resources to critical path activities to reduce duration, which increases cost but does not increase technical risk in the same way.

questions.question: Which of the following is an output of the planning phase?
questions.explanation: The project management plan is the primary output of the planning phase. It integrates all subsidiary plans (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resource, communication, risk, procurement, stakeholder). The charter is from initiation, deliverables from execution, and lessons learned from closing.

questions.question: A project has a CPI of 0.85 and an SPI of 1.10. What does this indicate?
options: The project is under budget and behind schedule
The project is over budget and ahead of schedule
The project is under budget and ahead of schedule
The project is over budget and behind schedule
questions.explanation: CPI (Cost Performance Index) of 0.85 means the project is getting only $0.85 of value for every $1 spent (over budget). SPI (Schedule Performance Index) of 1.10 means the project is completing 10% more work than planned (ahead of schedule). A CPI < 1 = over budget; SPI > 1 = ahead of schedule.

questions.question: During project execution, who is primarily responsible for managing and directing the project team?
questions.explanation: The project manager is responsible for managing and directing the project team during execution. This includes assigning work, monitoring performance, resolving conflicts, and ensuring deliverables meet quality standards. The sponsor provides oversight, not day-to-day management.

questions.question: Which risk response strategy involves shifting the negative impact of a risk to a third party?
questions.explanation: Transfer shifts the negative impact or ownership of a risk to a third party, typically through insurance, warranties, or contracts. Avoid eliminates the risk entirely, mitigate reduces the probability or impact, and accept acknowledges the risk without taking proactive action.

questions.question: A risk has a 30% probability of occurring and would cost $50,000 if it does. What is the expected monetary value (EMV) of this risk?
questions.explanation: Expected Monetary Value (EMV) = Probability x Impact = 0.30 x $50,000 = $15,000. EMV is used in quantitative risk analysis to calculate the weighted average of all possible outcomes, which helps in making reserve and risk response decisions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of obtaining formal acceptance of deliverables during the closing phase?
options: To begin the planning phase for the next project
To confirm that the project scope has been completed and the customer accepts the deliverables
questions.explanation: Formal acceptance confirms that the project deliverables meet the acceptance criteria defined in the scope statement and that the customer or sponsor is satisfied. Without formal acceptance, the project cannot be properly closed and there may be disputes later about deliverable completeness.

questions.question: Which of the following is typically included in a project charter?
options: Detailed project schedule with all task dependencies
High-level project description, objectives, success criteria, and assigned project manager
Final project budget with line-item detail
questions.explanation: A project charter includes the high-level project description, business justification, objectives, success criteria, high-level requirements, summary milestones, assigned project manager, and sponsor authority. Detailed schedules, budgets, and risk response plans are developed during the planning phase.

questions.question: What type of dependency exists when Activity B cannot start until Activity A finishes?
questions.explanation: Finish-to-start (FS) means the successor activity (B) cannot start until the predecessor activity (A) finishes. This is the most common type of dependency in project scheduling. Start-to-start means both start together, and finish-to-finish means both must finish together.

questions.question: What corrective action should a project manager take when variance analysis shows the project is significantly behind schedule?
options: Do nothing and wait for the project to self-correct
Evaluate schedule compression options such as crashing or fast tracking critical path activities
Change the project scope without stakeholder approval
questions.explanation: When a project is significantly behind schedule, the PM should evaluate schedule compression techniques like crashing (adding resources) or fast tracking (performing activities in parallel) on critical path activities. Any scope changes must go through formal change control.

questions.question: Bottom-up estimating is considered more accurate than analogous estimating because it:
options: Uses expert judgment from a single person
Estimates individual work packages and aggregates them into a total project estimate
Relies on historical data from similar projects
Uses mathematical models based on project parameters
questions.explanation: Bottom-up estimating breaks the project into work packages, estimates each one individually, and then aggregates them for the total. This granular approach is more accurate because it accounts for the specific details of each work package. It is also the most time-consuming estimating method.

questions.question: During execution, a team member informs the project manager that they will need two additional weeks to complete a critical task. What should the PM do FIRST?
options: Assess the impact on the critical path and overall project schedule before taking action
Remove the team member from the project
Ignore the delay since it is only two weeks
questions.explanation: The PM should first assess the impact of the delay. If the task is on the critical path, a two-week delay will extend the project. If not, the task may have float that absorbs the delay. After impact assessment, the PM can determine appropriate corrective actions.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT typically done during the closing phase?
questions.explanation: The project charter is created during the initiation phase, not the closing phase. Closing activities include releasing resources, conducting lessons learned sessions, archiving documents, obtaining formal sign-off, and closing contracts.

questions.question: What is a risk register?
options: A list of project team members and their skills
A document that records all identified risks, their analysis, and planned responses
A financial ledger for project expenses
questions.explanation: A risk register is a comprehensive document that records all identified risks along with their probability, impact, risk score, assigned owner, and planned response strategy. It is a living document that is updated throughout the project lifecycle.

questions.question: A project manager is using the parametric estimating technique. Which of the following is the BEST example?
options: Estimating the project will take the same time as a previous similar project
Calculating the cost based on $150 per square foot for 10,000 square feet of construction
Adding up individual task estimates from team members
Using the optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely values
questions.explanation: Parametric estimating uses a statistical relationship between historical data and other variables (a parameter) to calculate an estimate. Multiplying a known cost per unit ($150/sq ft) by the quantity (10,000 sq ft) is a classic parametric estimate. The first option describes analogous estimating, the third is bottom-up, and the fourth is three-point estimating.

questions.question: What does a negative cost variance (CV) indicate?
options: The project is ahead of schedule
questions.explanation: A negative Cost Variance (CV = EV - AC) means the project has spent more than the value of work completed, indicating it is over budget. A positive CV indicates under budget. CV measures cost performance, not schedule performance.

questions.question: During initiation, the project manager realizes the business case assumptions are no longer valid due to market changes. What is the BEST course of action?
options: Proceed with the project as originally planned
Escalate to the sponsor with updated information and recommend re-evaluating the project's viability
Cancel the project without informing anyone
Rewrite the project charter to match the new market conditions
questions.explanation: If the business case assumptions are no longer valid, the PM should escalate to the sponsor with the new information. The sponsor, who owns the business case, can then decide whether to modify the project scope, re-evaluate viability, or cancel. The PM should not make this decision unilaterally.

questions.question: What is the difference between quality assurance and quality control?
options: Quality assurance focuses on processes to prevent defects; quality control focuses on inspecting deliverables to find defects
Quality assurance and quality control are the same thing
Quality assurance is done only at the end of a project; quality control is done at the beginning
Quality assurance is the responsibility of the customer; quality control is the PM's responsibility
questions.explanation: Quality assurance (QA) focuses on the processes and procedures used to create deliverables, with the goal of preventing defects. Quality control (QC) focuses on inspecting the deliverables themselves to identify and correct defects. QA is proactive; QC is reactive.

questions.question: What is float (slack) in project scheduling?
options: The amount of time a non-critical activity can be delayed without delaying the project end date
The total duration of the project
The budget remaining at the end of the project
The number of resources assigned to a task
questions.explanation: Float (or slack) is the amount of time a non-critical activity can be delayed without affecting the project completion date. Activities on the critical path have zero float. Understanding float helps project managers prioritize which tasks need the closest monitoring.

questions.question: A project is being closed, but one team member insists on adding a feature the customer did not request, claiming it will improve the product. What should the PM do?
options: Allow the addition since it improves the product
Reject the addition as it constitutes gold plating and is not part of the approved scope
Add the feature but do not document it
Let the customer decide after the project is closed
questions.explanation: Adding unrequested features is called gold plating and is generally discouraged because it consumes resources, may introduce defects, and is not part of the approved scope. During closing, the focus should be on completing the defined deliverables, not adding new ones.

questions.question: Which risk response strategy is appropriate for a positive risk (opportunity)?
questions.explanation: Exploit is a risk response strategy for positive risks (opportunities). It involves taking actions to ensure the opportunity is realized. Avoid, mitigate, and transfer are strategies for negative risks (threats). Other positive risk strategies include enhance, share, and accept.

questions.question: What does a Schedule Performance Index (SPI) greater than 1.0 indicate?
options: The project is ahead of schedule
questions.explanation: An SPI (EV/PV) greater than 1.0 means the project is completing more work than planned for this point in time, indicating it is ahead of schedule. SPI = 1.0 means on schedule, and SPI < 1.0 means behind schedule. SPI measures schedule performance, not cost.

questions.question: Who typically signs and approves the project charter?
options: The project sponsor or an executive with sufficient authority
questions.explanation: The project charter is signed and approved by the project sponsor or an executive with sufficient organizational authority to authorize the project and allocate resources. The project manager is named in the charter but does not approve it.

questions.question: A project manager wants to determine the contingency reserve for a risk with three possible outcomes: 40% chance of $10,000 loss, 35% chance of $5,000 loss, and 25% chance of no impact. What is the appropriate contingency reserve?
questions.explanation: Contingency reserve = sum of EMVs = (0.40 x $10,000) + (0.35 x $5,000) + (0.25 x $0) = $4,000 + $1,750 + $0 = $5,750. This expected monetary value calculation provides a weighted average for setting the contingency reserve amount.

questions.question: During project execution, the PM discovers that a deliverable will not meet the quality requirements. What type of action should be taken?
questions.explanation: Defect repair is the appropriate action when a deliverable does not meet quality requirements. Corrective actions address process deviations, and preventive actions address potential future issues. Since the defect already exists in the deliverable, defect repair is the specific response needed.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a communication management plan?
options: To define who needs what information, when they need it, and how it will be delivered
To assign tasks to team members
questions.explanation: A communication management plan defines the communication requirements for the project, including what information is needed, who needs it, when they need it, how it will be delivered, and who is responsible for providing it. Effective communication is often cited as the most important project management skill.

questions.question: A project has 10 team members. How many communication channels exist?
questions.explanation: The formula for communication channels is n(n-1)/2, where n is the number of stakeholders. For 10 team members: 10(10-1)/2 = 10(9)/2 = 90/2 = 45 channels. This illustrates why communication complexity grows exponentially as team size increases.

questions.question: Which type of communication involves sending information to stakeholders without requiring immediate feedback?
questions.explanation: Push communication sends information to specific recipients without requiring immediate feedback (e.g., emails, memos, reports). Interactive communication involves real-time exchange (meetings, calls). Pull communication makes information available for recipients to access on their own (intranet, shared drives).

questions.question: What is the purpose of a change control board (CCB)?
options: To review, evaluate, approve, or reject change requests to the project
To manage the project budget only
questions.explanation: A change control board (CCB) is a formally established group responsible for reviewing, evaluating, approving, deferring, or rejecting changes to the project. The CCB ensures that changes are evaluated for their impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk before being implemented.

questions.question: A stakeholder submits a change request that would add three weeks to the schedule but significantly improve the product's market competitiveness. What should the project manager do?
options: Approve the change immediately since it improves the product
Reject the change since it impacts the schedule
Perform an impact assessment and present the findings with a recommendation to the CCB for a decision
Implement the change without documenting it
questions.explanation: All change requests must go through the formal change control process. The PM should assess the impact on all constraints (schedule, cost, scope, quality, risk), document the analysis, and present it to the CCB with a recommendation. The PM should not unilaterally approve or reject significant changes.

questions.question: A project team is distributed across three time zones. Which communication approach is MOST appropriate for routine status updates?
options: Require all team members to attend a real-time meeting at a time convenient for only one time zone
Use asynchronous communication methods such as email or shared dashboards, supplemented by periodic meetings at rotating times
Stop all communication and let team members work independently
Communicate only through the project sponsor
questions.explanation: For distributed teams, asynchronous communication (email, dashboards, recorded updates) ensures everyone can access information regardless of time zone. Supplementing with periodic real-time meetings at rotating times ensures fair participation. Requiring meetings at one time zone's convenience creates barriers for others.

questions.question: What is the FIRST step in the change control process when a change request is received?
options: Log the change request and perform an initial assessment
Notify all stakeholders that the project is changing
questions.explanation: The first step is to log the change request in the change log and perform an initial assessment of its impact. Every change request should be documented, regardless of whether it is ultimately approved or rejected. Implementation only occurs after proper evaluation and approval.

questions.question: If a project team grows from 5 to 8 members, how many additional communication channels are created?
questions.explanation: With 5 members: 5(4)/2 = 10 channels. With 8 members: 8(7)/2 = 28 channels. Additional channels = 28 - 10 = 18. This demonstrates why adding team members significantly increases communication complexity and why communication planning becomes more critical for larger teams.

questions.question: What is an impact assessment in the context of change management?
options: A financial audit of the project
An evaluation of how a proposed change will affect the project's scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk
A performance review of team members
questions.explanation: An impact assessment evaluates the effects of a proposed change on all aspects of the project including scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and resources. This analysis provides the CCB with the information needed to make an informed approval or rejection decision.

questions.question: What type of meeting typically occurs at regular intervals to share project progress and address issues?
questions.explanation: Status meetings occur at regular intervals (weekly, biweekly) to share progress updates, discuss issues, and coordinate upcoming work. Kickoff meetings happen once at the start, lessons learned at the end, and gate reviews at phase transitions.

questions.question: A project manager discovers that critical information is not reaching all relevant stakeholders. What should the PM review and update?
questions.explanation: If stakeholders are not receiving critical information, the communication management plan needs to be reviewed and updated. The plan defines communication channels, frequency, format, and responsible parties. Gaps in communication indicate the plan is incomplete or not being followed.

questions.question: A change request has been approved by the CCB. What should the project manager do NEXT?
options: Archive the change request with no further action
Update the project management plan, baselines, and communicate the change to affected stakeholders
Submit a new change request for the same change
Wait for the next status meeting to implement the change
questions.explanation: After a change is approved, the PM must update all affected project documents (management plan, baselines, schedules, budgets), communicate the change to affected stakeholders, and then execute the change. Simply archiving it or waiting defeats the purpose of the approval.

questions.question: What is the most effective method for resolving conflict between two team members who disagree on a technical approach?
options: Forcing one team member to accept the other's approach
Avoiding the conflict and hoping it resolves itself
Collaborating to find a solution that addresses both parties' concerns
Removing both team members from the project
questions.explanation: Collaboration (also called problem-solving or confronting) is the most effective conflict resolution technique because it addresses the root cause and finds a win-win solution. Forcing creates resentment, avoiding allows the conflict to fester, and removing team members is an extreme overreaction.

questions.question: What document tracks all change requests and their current status throughout the project?
questions.explanation: The change log (or change register) is a document that records all change requests along with their status (pending, approved, rejected, deferred, implemented). It provides a complete history of changes requested and their dispositions throughout the project.

questions.question: A project manager must deliver bad news to the project sponsor about a significant schedule delay. Which communication method is MOST appropriate?
options: Send an email to avoid confrontation
Mention it casually in a group meeting
Schedule a private face-to-face or video meeting to discuss the issue, its impact, and proposed solutions
Wait until the sponsor asks about the schedule
questions.explanation: Significant issues like schedule delays should be communicated directly and privately to the sponsor, preferably face-to-face or via video. This allows for immediate discussion, questions, and problem-solving. Email lacks nuance, group settings are inappropriate for sensitive news, and waiting is irresponsible.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of not having a formal change control process?
options: The project will always be completed on time
Uncontrolled scope creep, budget overruns, and schedule delays
The team will have less documentation to manage
questions.explanation: Without formal change control, changes can be made without proper evaluation of their impact, leading to scope creep, budget overruns, schedule delays, and quality issues. The change control process ensures all changes are assessed, approved, and tracked.

questions.question: Which meeting marks the official start of a project and introduces the team to the project goals and plan?
questions.explanation: The kickoff meeting marks the official start of a project (or phase). It introduces the project team, reviews the project goals and scope, discusses roles and responsibilities, and sets expectations for communication and collaboration.

questions.question: A project manager receives a verbal request from a senior executive to add a feature that is outside the approved scope. What is the correct response?
options: Implement the feature immediately since the request came from a senior executive
Politely ask the executive to submit a formal change request so it can be properly evaluated and processed through change control
Ignore the request since it was verbal
Add the feature but hide the cost in the contingency reserve
questions.explanation: All changes, regardless of who requests them, must go through the formal change control process. The PM should redirect the executive to submit a formal change request. This ensures the impact is assessed and the CCB can make an informed decision. No one is exempt from change control.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an escalation procedure in project communication?
options: To punish team members who miss deadlines
To define a clear process for elevating issues that cannot be resolved at the current level to higher authority
To send more emails to stakeholders
questions.explanation: Escalation procedures define a clear and agreed-upon process for elevating issues, risks, or decisions to higher levels of authority when they cannot be resolved at the current level. This ensures timely resolution of blockers and proper decision-making authority.

questions.question: After a change request is rejected by the CCB, what should the project manager do?
options: Document the rejection in the change log and notify the requestor with the rationale
Delete the change request from all records
Resubmit the same change request immediately
questions.explanation: Rejected change requests should be documented in the change log with the rationale for rejection, and the requestor should be notified. Maintaining this record provides an audit trail and prevents the same request from being resubmitted without new justification.

questions.question: A project manager is using a RACI matrix and notices that a task has no one listed as 'Accountable'. What risk does this create?
options: The task may not get completed because no one has final ownership or decision-making authority
Too many people will try to work on the task
The task will automatically be assigned to the sponsor
questions.explanation: If no one is designated as Accountable for a task, there is no single owner with final decision-making authority. This creates a risk that the task falls through the cracks, decisions are delayed, or no one takes responsibility for the outcome. Every task must have exactly one Accountable person.

questions.question: Which type of report provides a high-level summary of overall project health, often using indicators like red, yellow, and green?
questions.explanation: Dashboards and status reports use visual indicators (often red/yellow/green or RAG status) to provide a quick, high-level view of project health across key metrics like schedule, budget, scope, and risk. They are designed for busy stakeholders who need summary information.

questions.question: What type of chart displays project activities as horizontal bars along a timeline?
questions.explanation: A Gantt chart displays project activities as horizontal bars plotted against a timeline, showing start dates, end dates, durations, and dependencies. It provides a visual representation of the project schedule that is easy for stakeholders to understand.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a PERT chart over a Gantt chart?
options: PERT charts are easier to read
PERT charts show the dependencies and relationships between activities more clearly
PERT charts show the project budget
PERT charts eliminate the need for a project schedule
questions.explanation: PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) charts are network diagrams that clearly show activity dependencies and relationships, making it easier to identify the critical path. Gantt charts are better at showing timeline and duration but can be less clear about complex dependencies.

questions.question: A project manager needs to identify which activities have no scheduling flexibility. Which tool should be used?
questions.explanation: Critical path analysis identifies the longest path through the project network, showing which activities have zero float (no scheduling flexibility). Any delay on a critical path activity will delay the project end date. While Gantt charts can show the critical path, the analysis itself is the tool that identifies it.

questions.question: What is a project charter used for?
options: To define the detailed project schedule
To formally authorize the project and document the initial requirements and stakeholder expectations
questions.explanation: A project charter formally authorizes the existence of a project. It documents the project purpose, objectives, high-level requirements, milestones, assigned project manager, sponsor authority, and initial stakeholder expectations. It does not contain detailed schedules or daily tracking information.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Statement of Work (SOW) and a project charter?
options: The SOW describes the work to be performed, products, or services to be delivered; the charter formally authorizes the project and assigns the PM
The SOW is created after the charter by the project team
The charter describes the work in detail; the SOW authorizes the project
questions.explanation: The SOW is a narrative description of the work to be performed, the products or services to be delivered, and the acceptance criteria. The project charter formally authorizes the project and assigns the project manager with authority to use organizational resources. The SOW often precedes or accompanies the charter.

questions.question: A RACI matrix shows that three people are listed as 'Responsible' for the same task. What does this mean?
options: This is an error; only one person can be Responsible
All three people share the work of completing the task
All three people have decision-making authority
The task will be completed three times faster
questions.explanation: In a RACI matrix, multiple people can be listed as Responsible, meaning they all share in performing the work. However, only one person should be Accountable (the final decision-maker). Having multiple Responsible parties is valid, but the work distribution should be clearly defined.

questions.question: Which project management tool provides a centralized, visual summary of key project metrics and health indicators?
questions.explanation: A project dashboard provides a centralized, visual summary of key project metrics such as schedule status, budget status, risk levels, and milestone completion. Dashboards give stakeholders a quick at-a-glance view of overall project health.

questions.question: A project manager is creating a requirements traceability matrix (RTM). What is its PRIMARY purpose?
options: To ensure every requirement is linked to a deliverable and can be traced from origin through implementation and testing
questions.explanation: A requirements traceability matrix links each requirement to its origin, the deliverable that satisfies it, and the test that verifies it. This ensures no requirements are missed, all are implemented, and all are tested. It provides end-to-end traceability from business need to delivered product.

questions.question: Which tool is BEST suited for tracking issues, action items, and their resolution throughout a project?
questions.explanation: An issue log tracks identified issues, their assigned owner, priority, status, and resolution. It is the primary tool for ensuring issues are documented and addressed in a timely manner. Gantt charts track schedule, WBS defines scope, and network diagrams show dependencies.

questions.question: What does a scope statement define?
options: The project deliverables, boundaries, acceptance criteria, and exclusions
The names of all project team members
questions.explanation: The scope statement defines the project and product scope including deliverables, acceptance criteria, boundaries (what is included), exclusions (what is not included), constraints, and assumptions. It serves as a reference for making future scope decisions.

questions.question: A project manager notices that the same types of defects keep recurring during testing. Which quality tool would BEST help identify the most frequent causes?
questions.explanation: A Pareto chart ranks defects or causes by frequency, applying the 80/20 principle (80% of problems come from 20% of causes). It helps the PM prioritize which defect categories to address first for maximum quality improvement. The other tools serve different purposes unrelated to defect analysis.

questions.question: What is the WBS dictionary?
options: A glossary of project management terms
A document that provides detailed information about each WBS element including description, responsible party, and acceptance criteria
A list of all project acronyms
questions.explanation: The WBS dictionary provides detailed information about each element (work package) in the WBS, including the description of work, responsible party, schedule milestones, resource requirements, cost estimates, quality requirements, and acceptance criteria. It accompanies the WBS to provide the necessary detail.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of project management software?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Project, Jira, and Asana are project management software tools that help plan, schedule, track, and manage projects. They provide features such as task management, resource allocation, Gantt charts, and collaboration tools. The other options are productivity, design, and accounting software.

questions.question: A project manager needs to determine whether a project is financially viable. Which document typically contains the cost-benefit analysis and return on investment calculations?
questions.explanation: The business case contains the financial analysis including cost-benefit analysis, NPV, ROI, and payback period calculations that justify the project investment. It is typically created before the project charter and provides the rationale for project approval.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using a network diagram over a Gantt chart for complex projects?
options: Network diagrams are always simpler to create
Network diagrams better illustrate complex task dependencies, parallel paths, and the critical path
Network diagrams show resource assignments more clearly
questions.explanation: Network diagrams excel at showing complex task dependencies, multiple parallel paths, and the critical path. For projects with intricate dependency relationships, network diagrams provide clearer visualization than Gantt charts, which are better suited for showing timeline and duration information.

questions.question: A project is using a responsibility assignment matrix (RAM). What additional information does a RACI matrix provide beyond a basic RAM?
options: It adds the Consulted and Informed roles, clarifying communication and decision-making for each activity
questions.explanation: A basic RAM shows who is assigned to work on each task. A RACI matrix extends this by specifying four distinct roles: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the decision), Consulted (provides input before), and Informed (notified after). This clarifies both work assignments and communication responsibilities.

questions.question: What is a burndown chart used to track?
options: The remaining work or story points over time in an iteration or project
The number of team members on the project
The total number of change requests
questions.explanation: A burndown chart shows the amount of remaining work (often in story points or hours) plotted against time. The ideal line shows a steady decrease to zero by the end of the iteration. It is commonly used in Agile projects to track sprint or release progress.

questions.question: What is the purpose of meeting minutes in project management?
options: To record the duration of each meeting in minutes
To document decisions made, action items assigned, and key discussion points for accountability and reference
questions.explanation: Meeting minutes document what was discussed, decisions made, action items assigned (with owners and due dates), and key points raised during project meetings. They serve as an official record and accountability mechanism for follow-up.

questions.question: Which of the following is one of the four values of the Agile Manifesto?
options: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Following a plan over responding to change
questions.explanation: The Agile Manifesto values individuals and interactions over processes and tools. The other three values are: working software over comprehensive documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, and responding to change over following a plan. The items on the right still have value, but the left side is valued more.

questions.question: The Agile Manifesto states 'Responding to change over following a plan.' What does this mean in practice?
options: Plans should be abandoned at the first sign of change
While planning is important, Agile teams should be flexible and adapt to changing requirements and conditions
Change requests should always be rejected
questions.explanation: The Agile Manifesto values the ability to respond to change but does not say planning is unimportant. Agile teams still plan, but they recognize that plans must be flexible and adaptive. The manifesto states there is value in the items on the right (planning), but the items on the left (responding to change) are valued more.

questions.question: In the Scrum framework, what is a sprint?
options: A long-term project phase lasting several months
A time-boxed iteration, typically 1-4 weeks, during which the team delivers a potentially shippable product increment
A single task assigned to one developer
A meeting to discuss project risks
questions.explanation: A sprint is a time-boxed iteration in Scrum, typically lasting 1-4 weeks, during which the team works to deliver a potentially shippable product increment. Sprints have a fixed duration and cannot be extended. At the end of each sprint, the team has a working product increment.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a sprint retrospective?
options: To demonstrate completed work to stakeholders
To plan the next sprint's work
To reflect on the sprint process and identify improvements for the next sprint
questions.explanation: The sprint retrospective is a meeting at the end of each sprint where the team reflects on what went well, what could be improved, and what actions to take in the next sprint. The sprint review (not retrospective) demonstrates completed work. Sprint planning determines next sprint's work. Backlog refinement handles prioritization.

questions.question: In Scrum, the development team commits to delivering a set of user stories during sprint planning. Midway through the sprint, the product owner requests a new high-priority story be added. What should happen?
options: The new story is automatically added to the sprint
The sprint is cancelled and replanned
The Scrum master protects the sprint commitment; the new story goes to the product backlog for the next sprint unless the team agrees to a trade
The development team must work overtime to accommodate the addition
questions.explanation: Sprint scope should not change once committed. The Scrum master protects the team from mid-sprint scope changes. If the new story is critical, the team and product owner can negotiate removing an equivalent-sized story. Otherwise, it enters the product backlog for the next sprint. Forcing overtime violates Agile's sustainable pace principle.

questions.question: What is a product backlog in Scrum?
options: A list of bugs found during testing
A prioritized list of all features, requirements, enhancements, and fixes that constitute the work to be done on the product
A list of team member assignments
questions.explanation: The product backlog is a prioritized, dynamic list of everything that is known to be needed in the product. It includes features, requirements, enhancements, and bug fixes. The product owner is responsible for maintaining and prioritizing the backlog.

questions.question: What is velocity in Agile project management?
options: The speed at which the project manager makes decisions
The average number of story points or work units a team completes per sprint
The number of team meetings per week
questions.explanation: Velocity is a metric that measures the average number of story points (or other work units) a team completes per sprint. It is used for sprint planning and forecasting how much work the team can take on in future sprints. Velocity stabilizes over time as the team matures.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between Scrum and Kanban?
options: Scrum uses sprints with fixed iterations; Kanban uses continuous flow without prescribed iterations
Kanban requires a Scrum master; Scrum does not
Scrum does not use a backlog; Kanban does
They are identical frameworks with different names
questions.explanation: Scrum uses fixed-length sprints (iterations) with defined ceremonies, roles, and artifacts. Kanban focuses on continuous flow, limiting work-in-progress (WIP), and visualizing work on a board without prescribed iterations. Scrum is more prescriptive; Kanban is more flexible.

questions.question: What is a user story in Agile?
options: A short description of a feature from the end user's perspective, typically in the format 'As a [user], I want [feature], so that [benefit]'
A project charter for Agile projects
A bug report filed by a user
questions.explanation: A user story is a brief description of functionality from the end user's perspective. The common format is 'As a [type of user], I want [some goal], so that [some reason].' User stories are intentionally small and focused, serving as the basis for conversations about requirements.

questions.question: A team's velocity has been 40 story points per sprint for the last five sprints. The remaining product backlog contains 200 story points. How many sprints are needed to complete the backlog?
questions.explanation: Remaining work / velocity = 200 / 40 = 5 sprints. This assumes velocity remains stable at 40 points per sprint. Velocity-based forecasting is a key Agile planning technique, but it should be used as an estimate since velocity can vary from sprint to sprint.

questions.question: What is a hybrid methodology in project management?
options: Using only waterfall for all projects
Using only Agile for all projects
Combining elements of predictive (waterfall) and adaptive (Agile) methodologies to fit the project's needs
questions.explanation: A hybrid methodology combines elements from both predictive (waterfall) and adaptive (Agile) approaches to create a tailored methodology that fits the specific project needs. For example, a project might use waterfall for planning and Agile for development. This approach is increasingly common in real-world projects.

questions.question: In Kanban, what is the purpose of limiting Work in Progress (WIP)?
options: To give team members more free time
To reduce context switching, improve flow, and identify bottlenecks in the process
To eliminate the need for a product backlog
questions.explanation: WIP limits prevent the team from taking on too much work simultaneously. This reduces context switching, improves focus and quality, and makes bottlenecks visible. When a WIP limit is reached, the team must complete existing work before pulling new items, creating a pull-based system that optimizes flow.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a daily standup (daily Scrum)?
options: To provide a detailed progress report to management
To allow each team member to briefly share what they did yesterday, what they plan to do today, and any blockers
To assign new tasks to team members
To review and reprioritize the product backlog
questions.explanation: The daily standup (or daily Scrum) is a brief, time-boxed meeting (usually 15 minutes) where each team member shares three things: what they accomplished yesterday, what they plan to work on today, and any impediments blocking their progress. It promotes transparency and quick problem identification.

questions.question: A Scrum team consistently fails to complete all committed user stories by the end of each sprint. What should the team do?
options: Extend the sprint duration each time work is incomplete
Blame the product owner for prioritization issues
Use their actual velocity to commit to fewer story points in future sprints and investigate root causes during retrospectives
Add more team members to increase capacity
questions.explanation: The team should use their actual velocity (not aspirational targets) to plan future sprints. During retrospectives, they should investigate why stories are not being completed, such as poor estimation, unclear acceptance criteria, or external dependencies, and address root causes. Sprint duration should remain fixed.

questions.question: In Scrum, who is responsible for prioritizing the product backlog?
questions.explanation: The product owner is solely responsible for managing and prioritizing the product backlog. They determine the order in which items are developed based on business value, risk, dependencies, and stakeholder needs. The Scrum master facilitates the process, and the development team estimates the effort.
